title: C^0 Limits of Hamiltonian Paths and Spectral Invariants
speaker: Sobhan Seyfaddini
abstract: After reviewing spectral invariants, I will write down an estimate, which under certain assumptions, relates the spectral invariants of a Hamiltonian to the C0-distance of its flow from the identity. I will also show that, unlike the Hofer norm, the spectral norm is C0-continuous on surfaces. Time permitting, I will present an application to the study of area preserving disk maps.

proof_stages.label: C0 topology and rigidity context
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes notation for Hamiltonian paths, Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms, and C0 distances on maps and paths, then frames the talk as part of C0 symplectic rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Floer-theoretic spectral invariants
proof_stages.summary: Spectral invariants are introduced through the action functional on capped loops, the Floer chain complex, the quantum cohomology-to-Floer homology isomorphism, and the minimax action-level definition of rho(H;a).

proof_stages.label: Formal properties of spectral invariants
proof_stages.summary: The talk records the key tools: L^{(1,infinity)} Lipschitz continuity, behavior under adding a time function, the triangle inequality, spectrality under hypotheses, and homotopy invariance for normalized Hamiltonians.

proof_stages.label: Naive C0-continuity question and normalization obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The main continuity question for normalized Hamiltonians is posed, a shrinking-support counterexample shows it fails for the usual average normalization, and a replacement normalization by vanishing on a fixed ball is proposed.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-ball local Lipschitz theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that if two Hamiltonians vanish on a fixed ball, then sufficiently C0-close Hamiltonian paths have spectral invariants differing by at most a constant times their C0 path distance.

proof_stages.label: Epsilon-shift mechanism for the proof
proof_stages.summary: An epsilon-shiftability condition is defined, a support-shifting spectral estimate is stated, and a small Morse Hamiltonian with critical points inside the fixed ball is used to sketch why C0-small flows have small spectral invariant.

proof_stages.label: Cotangent ball bundle endpoint invariant
proof_stages.summary: For compactly supported Hamiltonians in a unit cotangent ball bundle, the Frauenfelder–Schlenk spectral invariant descends to the time-one map, and the same shifting idea yields C0 continuity on the Hamiltonian group.

proof_stages.label: Metric applications to Hofer and spectral norms
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares the C0, Hofer, and spectral norms, states a Hofer-level-set interior application for cotangent ball bundles, and states C0 continuity estimates for the spectral norm on surfaces.

examples.name: Gromov–Eliashberg C0 rigidity theorem
examples.note: The theorem that a C0 limit of symplectomorphisms is symplectic if it is smooth illustrates the kind of C0 rigidity phenomenon the talk studies.
examples.engagement_reason: the Gromov–Eliashberg theorem is stated and explained as a motivating rigidity phenomenon, but no proof or concrete application is carried out

examples.name: C-infinity-small autonomous Hamiltonian
examples.note: When the Floer complex agrees with the Morse complex, the speaker notes the computable case rho(f;1)=-min f, making the minimax definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the spectral invariant for a sufficiently C-infinity-small autonomous Hamiltonian, deriving that rho(f;1) equals minus the minimum of f

examples.name: Shrinking-support bump Hamiltonians
examples.note: Normalized bump functions whose supports shrink to a point have flows converging C0 to the identity but spectral invariants converging to an arbitrary constant, revealing that the standard average normalization is the obstruction and motivating fixed-ball normalization.
examples.engagement_reason: the shrinking-support bump Hamiltonians are used to build a counterexample, tracking their C0 convergence, displacement-energy bound, normalization, and limiting spectral invariant

passes.reason: The bump Hamiltonians have supports collapsing to a point and are explicitly used to disprove C0 continuity under standard normalization.
